glmassume 0.1.0
  • Reference
    • Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume

Plot Rootogram for Count Models

Source: R/plot_rootogram.R
plot_rootogram.Rd

Creates a hanging or standing rootogram for count models (Poisson or negative binomial). The rootogram compares observed and expected frequencies on a square-root scale, making it easier to detect departures from the assumed distribution.

Usage

plot_rootogram(
  model,
  style = c("hanging", "standing"),
  max_count = NULL,
  guide = getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE)
)

Arguments

model

A fitted glm object with family = poisson or a MASS::glm.nb() model.

style

Character. "hanging" (default) suspends observed bars from the expected curve; "standing" places observed bars on the x-axis.

max_count

Integer. Maximum count to display (default: auto-detected from the data). Counts beyond this are aggregated.

guide

Logical. Show interpretive subtitle? Defaults to getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE).

Value

A ggplot object.

Details

For a Poisson model, expected frequencies at count \(k\) are computed as sum(dpois(k, fitted(model))). For negative binomial models, they are sum(dnbinom(k, mu = fitted(model), size = model$theta)).

In a hanging rootogram, bars that extend below the x-axis indicate the model under-predicts that count; bars that stop above the axis indicate over-prediction.

References

Kleiber, C. and Zeileis, A. (2016). Visualizing Count Data Regressions Using Rootograms. The American Statistician, 70(3), 296–303.

Examples

counts <- c(18, 17, 15, 20, 10, 20, 25, 13, 12)
outcome <- gl(3, 1, 9)
treatment <- gl(3, 3)
m <- glm(counts ~ outcome + treatment, family = poisson)
plot_rootogram(m)
